coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots by chemical vapor deposition approach for use in polymer solar cell as the electrode buffer layer
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Lingpeng
Yang, Yongzhen
Ma, Chang-Qi
Liu, Xuguang
Wang, Hua
Xu, Bingshe
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs) with graphitic structure were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) for the first time. With this approach, the synthesized C-CQDs exhibit excellent crystaline graphitic nature with an average diameter of 3.5 nm and rich of hydrophobic CH3 terminal groups. Optical band gap of the C-CQDs was determined to be 3.16 eV, whereas low-lying HOMO/LUMO energy levels of −7.00/-3.84 eV were measured for these C-CQDs by cyclic voltammetry. Polymer solar cells using solution-processed C-CQDs as the electron transporting layer (ETL) were fabricated and tested. Results indicate the C-CQDs based devices exhibit similar or slightly improved device performance as the LiF based devices. Lowered series resistance (RS) was found for these C-CQDs based devices, suggesting a better interfacial connection between the Al electrode and polymer with this ETL. More importantly, improved thermal stability was confirmed for the CQD-based devices, which was ascribed to the less diffussion possibility of C-CQDs in solid film. The current work confirmed that the CVD synthesized C-CQDs could serve as a solution processed ETL for use in organic solar cells with improved device stability.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: C-CQDs as the ETLs for PSCs
Interfacial property analysis on the CQD based devices
Thermal stability of PSCs with different ETLs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials
Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology
Post-graduate Innovation Program of Shanxi Province
Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21176169 ), Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology ( 201513002-10 ), Post-graduate Innovation Program of Shanxi Province ( 20143010 ), Post-graduate Innovation Foundation of Taiyuan University of Technology ( S2014103 ) and Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology ( KLISEAM201502 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The proposed growth of C-CQDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
HRTEM images (a) and FTIR spectra (b) of synthesized C-CQDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-CQDs in solution, inset shows the dependence of (ahv)2 on hv for the C-CQDs, from which a band gap of 3.16 eV is deduced, (b) CV curve of C-CQDs (1 mg/mL) in 0.1 M TBAP supported DMF, at a scan rate of 100 mV/s. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Device structure and (b) Energy level diagram of the solar cells with C-CQDs as ETLs and P3HT:PC61BM, PTB7:PC61BM or PTB7-Th:PC71BM as the active layer. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
J–V curves (a) and EQE spectra (b) of P3HT:PC61BM based PSCs with different ETL. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
J–V curves of PTB7:PC61BM based PSCs with different ETL (a), EQE spectra of corresponding devices(b). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
J–V curves of PTB7-Th:PC71BM based PSCs with different ETL (a), EQE spectra of corresponding devices(b). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Dark J–V curves of P3HT: PC61BM based devices with different ETL. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Impedance spectra of P3HT: PC61BM based devices with different ETLs in the dark. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Normalized (a) V
OC , (b) J
SC , (c) FF, (d) PCE decay of P3HT:PC61BM solar cells with different ETLs at the temperature of 80 °C. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Characteristic parameters of P3HT:PC61BM based PSCs with different ETL.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots by chemical vapor deposition approach for use in polymer solar cell as the electrode buffer layer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Advanced Materials Science and Technology, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Advanced Materials Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Printable Electronics Research Center, Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionic, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionic
Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs) with graphitic structure were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) for the first time. With this approach, the synthesized C-CQDs exhibit excellent crystaline graphitic nature with an average diameter of 3.5 nm and rich of hydrophobic CH3 terminal groups. Optical band gap of the C-CQDs was determined to be 3.16 eV, whereas low-lying HOMO/LUMO energy levels of −7.00/-3.84 eV were measured for these C-CQDs by cyclic voltammetry. Polymer solar cells using solution-processed C-CQDs as the electron transporting layer (ETL) were fabricated and tested. Results indicate the C-CQDs based devices exhibit similar or slightly improved device performance as the LiF based devices. Lowered series resistance (RS) was found for these C-CQDs based devices, suggesting a better interfacial connection between the Al electrode and polymer with this ETL. More importantly, improved thermal stability was confirmed for the CQD-based devices, which was ascribed to the less diffussion possibility of C-CQDs in solid film. The current work confirmed that the CVD synthesized C-CQDs could serve as a solution processed ETL for use in organic solar cells with improved device stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the advantages of low cost, light weight, mechanical flexibility and solution processability, polymer solar cells (PSCs) have the potential to become new alternative sources of energy using spin-coating, inkjet printing, or roll-to-roll printing techniques to fabricate large area photovoltaic devices on flexible and light weight substrates [1–3]. To date, the main challenges for PSCs are efficiency and stability [4]. Significant improvements in efficiency have been achieved in PSCs through materials design [5–7], device optimization processing [8–10] and device interface engineering [11–13], and power conversion efficiency over 10% has been reported in the literature [14,15]. Long-term stability of PSCs is becoming the next key challenge for the successful commercialization of PSCs.

Typically, bulk heterojunction PSCs are fabricated by sandwiching a photoactive layer between a transparent conductive anode and a low work function metal cathode. Besides, electrode interfacial buffer layers are also essential for PSCs, because they play a critical role in improving device performance of PSCs [11–13]. Ideal interfacial buffer layer can facilitate charge extraction and prevent charge recombination at the interfaces between the photoactive layer and the cathode/anode electrodes, originating from a better energy alignment between these functional layers [16,17]. What's more, ideal interfacial buffer layer can also act as a barrier layer for limiting the diffusion of oxygen, moisture and Al atoms into the organic layers. Therefore, interfacial buffer layer can also improve device stability of PSCs. So far, LiF and Ca are the two most commonly used electron transport layers (ETLs) for PSCs and have been proved to be able to increase both the open circuit voltage (V OC) and fill factor (FF) of PSCs [16,18,19]. However, because of its insulating nature, the optimized LiF layer thickness is usually limited to few nanometers (1–2 nm), which is on one hand too thin to function as an effective protection barrier layer against oxygen and moisture [13,20], on the other hand, is more difficult to be precisely controlled during thermal evaporation. In addition, the fast diffusion rate of Li+ and F− would also cause the inferior device stability and operation lifetime [21,22]. Insertion of a thin Ca layer between Al electrode and the photoactive layer has been believed to form an ohmic contact and therefore result in an increase of device FF [23,24]. However, Ca is very sensitive to oxygen and moisture, which is a potential drawback for the stability of PSCs. In order to maximize the efficiency without posing detrimental effect on stability of PSCs, various kinds of inorganic semiconductors, such as ZnO [25], TiOx [26], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [27,28], and polymers [29,30] have been used as the ETLs in PSCs, and high device performance have been achieved for these ETL based devices.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as novel carbon nanomaterials, have been reported for use in solution processed photovoltaic devices as electron acceptor recently [31,32], due to their excellent photoelectric properties, solution processability, simple preparation and low cost [33,34]. Till now, CQDs reported in the literature were mostly synthesized via hydrothermal approach, which possess advantages of excellent chemical and thermal stability, low toxicity, intensive photoluminescence performance, low photobleaching and good biocompatibility. However, CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method (H-CQDs) usually contain multiple hydrophilic terminal groups or with long alkyl side chain, which might hinder the charge transport in the blended film. Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is an alternative method to synthesize carbon nanomaterials with precise CQD property control, however, there is no report on the synthesis of CQDs by CVD approach till now. In this paper, we report the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs) via a simple and fast CVD method for the first time. The synthesized C-CQDs have crystalline graphite structure with an average diameter of 3.5 nm. The synthesized C-CQDs can be used as the ETL in polymer solar cells. Results showed that the C-CQDs-based devices have similar device performance as the LiF based devices, whereas improved thermal stability was found for the C-CQD-based cells when compared to LiF based devices, which was attributed to lower molecular diffusion rates of C-CQDs. The current work proved that C-CQDs prepared by CVD approach could be an alternative ETL materials for polymer solar cells.

Highly pure C2H2 and Ar (>99.99%) were purchased from Leap Gas Co. Ltd. N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents. n-Hexane and cyclohexane were supplied by Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co. Ltd. Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS; Clevios PVP AI 4083) was purchased from Heraeus Precious Metals GmbH & Co. KG. Regioregular poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT; Mn = 5.0 × 104 g/mol, PDI = 1.7, regioregularity Rr = 95%) was purchased from Solarmer Energy, Inc. (Beijing). Poly[[4,8-bis[(2-ethylhexyl]oxy]-benzo[1,2-b:4,5-b′] dithiophene-2,6-diyl][3-fluoro-2-[(2-ethylhexyl)-carbonyl]thieno[3,4-b]thiophenediyl]] (PTB7) and poly[[2,6′-4,8-di(5-ethylhexylthienyl)benzo[1,2-b;3,3-b′]dithiophene][3-fluoro-2[(2-ethylhexyl)carbonyl]thieno[3,4-b]thiophenediyl]] (PTB7-Th) were purchased from 1-Material and Solarmer Materials Inc., respectively. Phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM) and [6,6]-phenyl-C71-butyric acid methyl ester (PC71BM) were purchased from Solenne B.V. H-CQDs were prepared following the literature [35].

C-CQDs were synthesized by CVD in Ar atmosphere using C2H2 as carbon source (see Fig. 1 for the experiment set-up). The preparation was conducted in a quartz tube (32 mm i.d. and 1 m long), which was mounted in a horizontal tubular furnace. At the beginning of synthesis, the quartz tube was heated to 1000 °C in Ar atmosphere. Then a mixture of C2H2/Ar was introduced into the reactor at a rate of 70/700 mL min−1 for 2 h. After the reaction, the quartz tube was cooled to room temperature under the protection of flowing Ar. The crude product was collected in a receiving tank, and dissolved in 200 mL of DMF by sonication for 30 min. The DMF solution was filtered through a filter paper (in middle speed) and syringe filter (Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), 0.45 μm). The organic solvents were removed under vacuum to give the C-CQDs.

Photovoltaic devices were fabricated with the configuration of ITO/PEDOT:PSS/active layer/ETL/Al. The patterned substrates were cleaned in an ultrasonic bath using detergent, deionized water, acetone and isopropanol in sequence. Immediately prior to device fabrication, the substrates were treated in a UV-ozone oven for 30 min. PEDOT:PSS, filtered through a syringe filter (nylon, 0.45 μm), was spin-coated on the cleaned and patterned ITO substrates at 3500 rpm for 60 s. The samples were subsequently annealed at 120 °C for 10 min on a hot plate. For P3HT:PC61BM based solar cells, the active layers composed of P3HT:PC61BM (1:1, w/w) were spin coated from a mixture solution of P3HT and PC61BM in 1,2-dichlorobenzene (o-DCB, with a total concentration of 40 mg/mL) at 600 rpm for 60 s. Subsequently, the active layers were annealed at 120 °C for 10 min in N2 glove box. For PTB7:PC61BM based solar cells, the PTB7 and PC61BM were first dissolved in chlorobenzene at a weight ratio of 1:1.5 (with a total concentration of 25 mg/mL) with addition of 3% volume ratio of 1,8-diiodooctane (DIO). Then the mixture solution was spin-coated onto the top of the ZnO layer at 1000 rpm for 60 s. For LiF based solar cells, LiF (1.5 nm) and Al (100 nm) were sequentially vacuum-deposited on the top of the active layer at pressure about 8 × 10−5 Pa. For C-CQDs based solar cells, C-CQDs with different concentration (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 mg/mL in methanol) was spin-coated on the active layers at 2000 rpm for 60 s, and Al (100 nm) were sequentially vacuum-deposited on the top of the C-CQDs layer as the anode. The effective area of the devices was 0.09 cm2.

Devices were processed, characterized and aged in glove box filled with inert nitrogen gas. The long-term stability measurement of un-encapsulated devices was conducted using a solar cell performance decay test system (PVLT-G9100, Suzhou D&R Instruments) under a testing condition in accordance with ISOS-L-1. In detail, the un-encapsulated devices were put inside a glove box (H2O < 10 ppm, O2 < 10 ppm), and continuously illuminated with LED white light (color temperature, 6000 K). The illumination light intensity was initially set as the output device short circuit current equals to J
SC measured at standard condition. The illumination light intensity was monitored by a photodiode (Hamamtsu S1336-8BQ). Maximum power outputs (mpp) of these devices were checked periodically according to the J–V measurement, and the devices were attached with an external load at the maximum power output point under illumination. The environmental temperature, actual cell temperature and photovoltaic performances of devices (V
OC , J
SC , FF, PCE, R
s , and R
sh ) were continuously monitored and recorded every 30 min.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the C-CQDs were taken from an FEI Tecani G2 F20 S-Twin 200 kV microscope. Fourier transformation infrared spectra (FTIR) of C-CQDs were characterized by a Tenror 27 FTIR spectrometer. The U-3900 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–Vis) and Fluoromax-4 photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer were used to characterize the optical property of the C-CQDs. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve was obtained in a tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6, 0.1 mol L−1) supported DMF solution at room temperature using a RST3000 electrochemical workstation operated at a scanning rate of 100 mV s−1, in which a Pt wire (ϕ = 1 mm) embedded in Teflon column was used as the working electrode, a Pt sheet and a Ag/AgCl electrode were served as the counter and reference electrode, respectively, and ferrocene/ferrocenium was used as the internal reference to calibrate the redox potentials. The current density–voltage (J–V) characteristics were measured in a N2-filled glove box using a Keithley 2400 source meter under an AM 1.5G filter (100 mW cm2) generated by white light from halogen tungsten lamp, filtered by a Schott GG385 UV filter and a Hoya LB120 daylight filter. External quantum efficiency (EQE) was measured under simulated one sun operation condition using bias light from a 532 nm solid state laser. Light from a 150 W tungsten halogen lamp (Osram 64610) was used as probe light and modulated with a mechanical chopper before passing the monochromator (Zolix, Omni-k300) to select the wavelength. The response was recorded as the voltage by an IV converter (DNR-IV Convertor, Suzhou D&R Instruments), using a lock-in amplifier (Stanford Research Systems SR 830). A calibrated Si cell was used as reference. The device for EQE measurement was kept behind a quartz window in a nitrogen filled container.

The synthesis of C-CQDs is rather simple and fast, and it belongs to a typical “bottom-up” method. A possible formation mechanism is schematically illustrated in Fig. 2 . The formation of C-CQDs consists of steps of cracking, nucleation and growth. Initially, Ar is introduced into the reactor to purge air at the temperature below 1000 °C. As the quartz tube is heated to 1000 °C with a mixture of C2H2/Ar being introduced into the reactor, C2H2 starts to dissociated into H atom, C atom and C species such as CHx (x = 0–3) [36], as called “cracking”. In the following “nucleation” stage, upon the dissociation of C2H2, the concentration of C atom and C species increases. With the influence of high temperature and pressure, C atom and C species collide with each other and aggregate. C species grow up and nucleate as the reaction proceeds. The nucleated C species are of high crystallinity because of the reaction temperature as high as 1000 °C. In the third “growth” stage, a polymerization takes place in an inert atmosphere (Ar), and the flowing Ar carries the C species to the end of the quartz tube and a receiving flask. The nucleated C species adsorb other C atoms or species on the surface and realize polymerization during cooling. As the reaction proceeds, the C particles grow larger, leading to the formation of C-CQDs.

Fig. 3 a shows the HRTEM image of C-CQDs. The C-CQDs show well-resolved lattice fringes with interplanar spacing of 0.20 and 0.27 nm, corresponding respectively to the (102) and (020) diffraction facets of sp2 graphite carbon [34,37], respectively, implying that the C-CQDs are structured like crystalline graphite carbon. Moreover, the electron diffraction pattern shown in the inset at the bottom right corner of Fig. 3a also indicates the polymorphic nature of the C-CQDs. According to the statistical particle size distribution, the typical size of the C-CQDs is in the range of 2–7 nm, with an average diameter of 3.5 nm. The surface functional groups of the C-CQDs were characterized by FTIR (Fig. 3b). The C-CQDs show relatively weak OH vibration signal, but intensive CH stretching signals between 2850 and 3000 cm−1 and vibration of CH3 at 1456 and 1377 cm−1 [38], demonstrating that the C-CQDs have more hydrophobic CH or CH3 terminal groups. In addition, intensive CC stretching at 1271 and 1668 cm−1 can be found, suggesting the graphitic structure of C-CQDs, in good agreement with the TEM results. Excitation-dependent PL spectra of C-CQDs are given in Fig. S1 (in supporting information). Under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation, the C-CQDs exhibit bright blue-green emission, while no further passivation step is required for PL to occur, as shown in the photograph of C-CQDs solution (inset at the bottom right corner of Fig. S1).

To demonstrate the energy levels of C-CQDs, UV–Vis absorption and cyclic voltammetry measurements were carried out. Fig. 4 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-CQDs in DMF (0.1 mg/mL) solution. As can be seen here, C-CQDs show strong optical absorption in ultraviolet region tailing upto 600 nm. By extrapolating the (ahv)2-hv (where a: absorbance, h: Plank's constant, and v: frequency) curve, the band gap (E g) of the C-CQDs was estimated to be 3.16 eV (Fig. 4a, insertion), which is similar to the value reported in the literature synthesized by electrochemical etching method [39]. Fig. 4b shows the CV curve of C-CQDs. As can be seen here, the C-CQDs exhibit apparently a reversible reduction process. The HOMO/LUMO energy levels were then estimated from the onset reduction potential according to Equation (1) as follows [40]:
(1) LUMO = - (E o red +5.10) (eV)
(2)
E
g = LUMO−HOMO
where E o red is the onset reduction potential. The E o red was determined to be −1.26 V, and the LUMO level was calculated to be −3.84 eV, which is slight higher than that of PC61BM (−4.01 eV) [41]. The HOMO value of C-CQDs was then calculated to be −7.00 eV according to Equation (2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-CQDs as the ETLs for PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Polymer solar cells (PSCs) with a device structure of ITO/PEDOT:PSS/P3HT (or PTB7):PC61BM/C-CQDs/Al were fabricated and tested, where the blended layer of P3HT (or PTB7) with PC61BM functions as the photoactive layer, and the solution deposited CQD layer serves as the electron transporting layer (ETL, Fig. 5 a). The CQD layer was optimized by varying the concentration of CQD solution for deposition, and results showed that CQD concentration can be manipulated over a large range of 0.05–0.2 mg/mL with only slightly device performance variation (see more details in Supporting information), indicating less CQD-layer thickness dependence of these CQD based devices. Devices based on CQD layer deposited from 0.05 and 0.1 mg/mL solution are selected for the following discussion part. For comparison, reference cells with LiF as the ETL or without ETL were also fabricated and tested. Fig. 5b shows the energy level diagram of various function layers. As can be seen here, the LUMO energy level of C-CQDs is slightly higher (0.17 eV) than that of PC61BM. However, the reasonable high photovoltaic performance of the CQD-based solar cells demonstrated that such an energy barrier does not hamper the electron injection between PC61BM and CQD (vide infra).

Fig. 6 depicts the J-V curves and EQE spectra of P3HT:PC61BM devices. The key parameters of the best devices, including open circuit voltage (V
OC ), short circuit current (J SC), fill factor (FF), and power conversion efficiency (PCE) are summarized in Table 1 . The average PCE with standard deviation calculated over 4 individual cells are also listed in this table for comparison. As can be seen here, devices without ETLs exhibit a poor device performance with an average PCE of 1.60% (entry 1 in Table 1). The devices with LiF as the ETLs showed an enhanced PCE of 2.91% (entry 3 in Table 1), which is comparable for P3HT:PC61BM based devices with a conversional device structure reported in the literature [42]. The CQD incorporated devices showed slightly improved device performance in comparison with the LiF based device (entry 4 and 5 vs. entry 3), indicating that C-CQDs could serve as solution processable ETL in polymer solar cells. In order to exclude the solvent effect for such device performance improvement, reference devices based on methanol treated P3HT:PC61BM layer were fabricated and tested (entry 2 in Table 1). Results showed that methanol treatment leads to a 44% enhancement in PCE in comparison with the ETL-free devices, which was ascribed to surface traps passivation, and the consequent increased mobility, accelerated charge extraction and reduced charge recombination [10]. Although the device performance is improved after methanol treatment, it is still lower than that of the devices using solution-processed C-CQDs as the ETL. This indicates that C-CQDs play an important role in such PSCs performance enhancement. For P3HT:PC61BM devices, the incorporation of CQD ETLs leads to a highest PCE of 3.11% with a V
OC of 0.63 V, J
SC of 9.49 mA/cm2 and FF of 51%. The improvement of PCE is on one hand due to the increase of J SC, which can be also clearly seen from the external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra (Fig. 6b), one the other hand due to the enhanced FF, which is attributed to improved electron-extracting and hole-blocking ability of the C-CQDs (vide infra).

The applicability of the C-CQDs was also tested in PTB7:PC61BM based devices. Fig. 7 shows the J-V curves and EQE spectra of the devices with different ETLs, and the characteristic parameters are summarized in Table 2 . As can be seen here, the CQD incorporated devices (entry 9 and 10) show similar device performance as the LiF based devices (entry 8). However, improved device performance was found for the CQD based devices in comparison with devices without ETL or methanol treated reference cells. In details, the PTB7:PC61BM devices with CQD ETLs exhibit an improved FF (0.67 vs. 0.61) and PCE (6.85% vs. 6.28%) in comparison with the ETL-free devices, suggesting that CQD indeed serves as the ETL in these devices.

To further investigate the feasibility of C-CQDs for use as ETL in polymer solar cells, BHJ solar cells based on PTB7-Th:PC71BM using different ETL were fabricated and tested. Fig. 8 depicts the J-V curves and EQE spectra of the PTB7-Th:PC71BM cells with different ETLs, and the characteristic performance parameters are summarized in Table 3 . As can be seen here, the C-CQDs based cells (entry 14 and 15) showed comparable device performance as the LiF based devices (entry 13). Obviously, C-CQDs based devices show improved device performance in comparison with that without ETL or methanol treated reference cells (entry 11 and 12), demonstrating that the C-CQD layer function as the ETL in these devices. In addition, PTB7-Th:PC71BM devices incorporated with H-CQDs ETLs were also fabricated and tested (entry 16 and 17) for comparison. Results show that H-CQDs based devices also showed slightly improved device performance when compared to device without ETL, indicating that H-CQDs could also serve as ETL in polymer solar cells. However, the H-CQDs based devices show slightly lower device performance in comparison with the C-CQDs based devices. That could be due to the more hydrophilic terminal groups as well as more surface defects of the H-CQDs, which hinder the charge transport in devices and result in worse device performance. Nevertheless, these experiments confirmed that the C-CQDs can be used as ETL in various polymer solar cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interfacial property analysis on the CQD based devices
ce:section.ce:para:
To obtain a deep insight into the working mechanism of C-CQDs in PCSs, the dark J-V curves of P3HT:PC61BM based devices were analyzed according to the classic one-diode model [43], with which the J-V characteristics of organic solar cells can be described as the following equation [44]:
(3)
J =
J 0
{
exp
[
q
(
V − J
R
s h
)
n
k B
T
]
− 1
}
+
V − J
R s
R
s h
−
J
PH
where J
0 , q, n, R
s , R
sh , k
B , T and J
PH are the reverse saturation current density in the dark, elementary charge, diode ideal factor, series resistance, shunt resistance, Boltzmann constant, absolute temperature, and photo-generated current density, respectively. Under dark condition, the photo-generated current density is assumed to be zero (J
PH = 0), and thus the experimental J-V curves are fitted to Eq. (3) according to the method reported in the literature [45,46]. Fig. 9 shows the double logarithmic dark J-V curves of the P3HT:PC61BM based devices with different ETLs. The fitting curves are shown in Fig. S2 (in supporting information), and the fitted parameters are listed in Table 1.

As can be seen from Table 1, the CQD based PSCs show similar J
0 , n, R
s and R
sh with LiF based devices. However, lower J
0 , n, and R
s was found for the CQD based device in comparison with the PSCs without ETLs. The lower J
0 of C-CQDs based device suggests better electron injection and hole blocking properties of the C-CQDs layer, functioning electron transporting layer as LiF layers. Furthermore, the n of C-CQDs based device is determined to be around 1.5, which is smaller than that of the devices without ETLs (1.71), indicating less charge recombination [45]. In addition, lower R
s of the CQD based devices suggest a good ohmic contact between P3HT:PC61BM/C-CQDs/Al interfaces. However, devices with C-CQDs as ETLs show slightly smaller R
sh than devices without ETLs, which might be ascribed to the defects on the surface of C-CQDs that may result in some trap-assisted recombination. Nevertheless, the dark J-V curve analysis results showed that CQD based devices show similar device parameter as the LiF based ones, suggesting CQD could serve ETL in polymer solar cell as an alternative of LiF.

The impedance spectra of PSCs with different ETLs in the dark were also measured. The impedance spectra with frequency ranging from 1 Hz to 1.9 MHz are shown in Fig. 10 . The shapes of impedance spectra are all semicircles, and the series resistance of PSCs is closely related to the diameter of the semicircles. The semicircle's diameter in the diagram stands for the impedance values, and lager diameter means lager impedance [47,48]. From the diagram, it is clearly shown that the semicircle's diameter of devices without ETLs is much larger than that of devices with C-CQDs as ETLs, suggesting that introduction of C-CQDs as ETLs in devices forms a better interface, and thus the injection barrier is decreased. Thus, the introduction of C-CQDs as ETLs brings a significant decline to the device impedance, which contributes to the improvement of FF and increase of J
SC . According to the data in Table 1, the series resistance (RS) of the optimized device is calculated as 9.9 Ω cm2, which is much smaller than the PSCs without ETLs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of PSCs with different ETLs
ce:section.ce:para:
Chen et al. recently demonstrated that C-CQDs synthesized via hydrothermal approach could improve the long term stability of polymer solar cells at room temperature [49]. In this work, thermal stability of PSCs with various ETL was investigated at 80 °C. Such a measurement could better simulate the degradation behavior of PSCs, since the surface temperature could reach as high as 80 °C during operation, on the other hand, such an accelerate aging test could shorten the necessary testing procedure. Fig. 11 shows the evolution of V
OC , J
SC , FF and PCE of un-encapsulated P3HT:PC61BM PSCs with different ETLs. Note that the initial device performance of these P3HT:PC61BM cells are comparable (Table 1). In addition, the degradation behaviors were tested inside the glove box with low oxygen and moisture contents, therefore, the decay curves showed here are more ETL-related intrinsic behavior. As can be seen here, the LiF based device showed stable V OC, fast decrease of J SC and slightly increase of FF, with an overall fast decay of power conversion efficiency. The lifetime T80 (time at 80% of its initial efficiency) of this cell was determined to be 42 h. The CQD-based devices showed stable V OC, but slightly decrease of FF during aging. More significantly, slightly increase of J SC was found for those CQD based devices during aging, leading to a much slower decay of these cells. It is already known that both Li+ and F- diffuse quite fast in organic layer [21], which was ascribed to the reason for the performance decay of PSCs. The stability improvement of CQD based device could therefore be attributed to the less diffusion possibility of C-CQDs, owing to its large molecule dimensionality. On the other hand, the initial J SC increase for the C-CQDs based devices could be attributed to the improvement of interface contact between the C-CQDs and active layer, and/or the C-CQDs and the aluminum electrode during aging at 80 °C. The T80 of the CQD based device was determined to be more than 150 h, which is more 3 times longer than that of LiF based device. Clearly, C-CQDs could serve as ETL in polymer solar cell with excellent thermal stability.

In conclusion, C-CQDs with graphitic structure can be successfully synthesized by CVD method. The application of these C-CQDs in polymer solar cells as the ETLs was successfully demonstrated. Optimized PCE of P3HT:PC61BM, PTB7:PC61BM and PTB7-TH:PC71BM devices using CQD ETLs reached 3.11%, 6.85% and 8.23% respectively, which are comparable with the LiF based devices. The high device performance mainly originates from the better electron injection and reduced interface resistance caused by CQD ETLs. In addition, a wide tolerance of CQD-ETL layer thickness was demonstrated, which is conducive to the realization of large scale roll-to-roll printing processing. More importantly, the long-term thermal stability of devices with C-CQDs as ETLs was also improved significantly, which was ascribed to less molecule diffusion of C-CQDs. Overall, we demonstrated here that a new type of carbon nanomaterial, C-CQDs that have advantages of environmentally friendly, easily processable, stable and low cost can be used as ETL as the alternative of LiF in polymer solar cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21176169), Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology (201513002-10), Post-graduate Innovation Program of Shanxi Province (20143010), Post-graduate Innovation Foundation of Taiyuan University of Technology (S2014103) and Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology (KLISEAM201502).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.08.058.
